coredata.dc:title: Spontaneous imbibition characteristics of carbon nanofluids in carbonate reservoirs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Sakthivel, Sivabalan
Kanj, Mazen Yousef
coredata.dc:description:
Altering the wettability of the rock–oil–brine system plays a substantial role in the oil recovery process from carbonate reservoirs. Hence, there arises a need for understanding the wetting and wettability alteration of this system. In this study, we ran a suite of spontaneous imbibition tests and investigated the efficiency of Carbon nanodots (CND) and an in-house cationic Gemini surfactant (GS8) on the wettability alteration of and recovery from oil-wet carbonate rock samples. The study considered the effects of additives concentrations, sample aging, initial water saturation, and rock permeability. Both CND and GS8 proved efficient at concentrations below 200 ppm. At 200 ppm, separately CND in seawater and GS8 in seawater demonstrated each about 22%–25% incremental oil recovery over neat seawater. Low field nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) measurements supported this observation. NMR
T
2
distribution and NMR
T
2
D fluid typing measurements helped evaluate the recovery in terms of the pore size distribution and the oil and brine water saturation at different imbibition stages. CND exhibited a strong positive effect on the wettability of all tested samples, and accordingly, on the imbibition characteristics and oil recovery. It altered the wettability from strongly oil-wet to water-wet with equal efficiency in all pore systems making it a strong candidate for enhanced oil recovery (EOR). GS8, on the other hand, was most efficient in the macropores. Unlike CND, the efficiency of GS8 decreased in the mesopores and more so in the micropores. Rock–oil–brine surface contact angle and oil–brine interfacial tension measurements supported the imbibition test results and helped interpret the recovery mechanism. Overall, CND proved effective for EOR in the harsh carbonate reservoir environment and demonstrated itself as a viable and economical substitute to the more costly and complex oilfield chemicals for recovery operations.

head.ce:title: Spontaneous imbibition characteristics of carbon nanofluids in carbonate reservoirs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Altering the wettability of the rock–oil–brine system plays a substantial role in the oil recovery process from carbonate reservoirs. Hence, there arises a need for understanding the wetting and wettability alteration of this system. In this study, we ran a suite of spontaneous imbibition tests and investigated the efficiency of Carbon nanodots (CND) and an in-house cationic Gemini surfactant (GS8) on the wettability alteration of and recovery from oil-wet carbonate rock samples. The study considered the effects of additives concentrations, sample aging, initial water saturation, and rock permeability. Both CND and GS8 proved efficient at concentrations below 200 ppm. At 200 ppm, separately CND in seawater and GS8 in seawater demonstrated each about 22%–25% incremental oil recovery over neat seawater. Low field nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) measurements supported this observation. NMR
T
2
distribution and NMR
T
2
D fluid typing measurements helped evaluate the recovery in terms of the pore size distribution and the oil and brine water saturation at different imbibition stages. CND exhibited a strong positive effect on the wettability of all tested samples, and accordingly, on the imbibition characteristics and oil recovery. It altered the wettability from strongly oil-wet to water-wet with equal efficiency in all pore systems making it a strong candidate for enhanced oil recovery (EOR). GS8, on the other hand, was most efficient in the macropores. Unlike CND, the efficiency of GS8 decreased in the mesopores and more so in the micropores. Rock–oil–brine surface contact angle and oil–brine interfacial tension measurements supported the imbibition test results and helped interpret the recovery mechanism. Overall, CND proved effective for EOR in the harsh carbonate reservoir environment and demonstrated itself as a viable and economical substitute to the more costly and complex oilfield chemicals for recovery operations.

ce:section.ce:para: Wettability is an essential petrophysical property. It plays a significant role in the oil recovery mechanism and controls the imbibition response of the fluid in the reservoir. It impacts the relative permeability and capillarity characteristics of the matrix and influences the distribution of fluids within the porous media (Wang and Gupta, 1995; Nilsson et al., 1997).

Spontaneous imbibition is a capillary driven physical process in which the wetting fluid is drawn into the porous matrix stimulated through the surface energy, translated as the driving pressure, and opposed by the viscous force. The rate of the imbibition process is usually determined by the capillary pressure and viscous forces. It is further controlled by rock lithology, porosity, permeability, wettability, fluid type, fluid composition, viscosity, interfacial tension, temperature, and pressure, among others. Wettability and capillarity dominate this process (Andersen and Ahmed, 2020; Andersen et al., 2019; Cai et al., 2020; Morrow et al., 1994).

Oil recovery by waterflooding is highly dominated by capillary forces. Once there is a favorable positive capillary pressure, water would imbibe into the rock matrix and expel the oil. In a typical spontaneous imbibition process, the experiments are performed using the Amott cell setup. In this setup, oil-saturated samples at the initial or irreducible formation water (FW) saturation are immersed in the imbibing fluid, and oil production is recorded over time to provide a feel of the average wettability of the samples. The Amott cell experiment is one of the conventional benchmark methods of the oil industry in evaluating newly formulated oilfield chemicals. During this process, imbibing water is drawn into the rock matrix based on the rock’s native wettability. If the rock is water-wet, the imbibition rate is fast, and the oil recovery is high. For oil-wet or mixed-wet samples, the imbibition rate is slower with lower recovery (Zhou et al., 2000; Alvarez and Schechter, 2017).

There are several well-studied methods for enhanced oil recovery (EOR) methods targeting the oil-wet carbonate formation. Chemical EOR (cEOR) is one such method that gained attention in the last three decades (Davarpanah and Mirshekari, 2020; Ebadati et al., 2019). It is a leading candidate EOR method for Saudi Arabian carbonate reservoirs. Trapped oil by microscopic capillary forces and bypassed oil by macroscopic heterogeneities are the two fraction oils that are left in the porous media after waterflooding. Surfactants reduce the oil/water interfacial tension (IFT) and support the wettability alteration and oil mobilization. Surfactants and polymers are often used in tandem. Polymers are injected after surfactants to avoid viscous fingering and create better sweep performance (Sakthivel et al., 2016a, 2017). However, most oilfield chemicals are challenged by harsh reservoir conditions. High pressure and high salinity conditions cause chemicals to precipitate and damage the reservoir (Sakthivel et al., 2016a, 2017; Sakthivel and Velusamy, 2020; Sakthivel et al., 2016b; Velusamy et al., 2017; Sakthivel et al., 2014a,b).

Most chemicals are prone to adsorption in the near-wellbore region and cannot travel far into the reservoir. More importantly, oilfield chemicals are often used in high concentrations (above 1 w/v% or 10,000 ppm), severely limiting their applicability and feasibility in field operations and restricting their use to lab scale testing or small scale field piloting. Surfactants are commonly used in cEOR to alter the rock wettability, reduce the oil–water interfacial tension, and increase the oil production. In the past, many studies recognized different type of surfactants, such as cationic, anionic, and non-ionic (Standnes and Austad, 2000a; Austad and Standnes, 2003; Adibhatla and Mohanty, 2008; Sharma and Mohanty, 2013; Gupta and Mohanty, 2010; Kathel and Mohanty, 2013; Standnes and Austad, 2000b). Typically, the anionic surfactants are preferentially used in sandstone reservoirs. The negatively charged sandstone surface will not adsorb the negatively charged surfactant. Similarly, cationic surfactants make a good choice for carbonate reservoirs. However, the mechanism for the wettability alteration differ with the type of surfactants and rocks. Standnes and Austad (2000) postulated that there is an ion-pair mechanism taking place between the cationic surfactant and the anionic acidic oil component that are adsorbed on the carbonate rock surface (Standnes and Austad, 2000b). Generally, the selection of the anionic surfactant for a carbonate reservoir is considered to be the last choice due to the chemical retention. Albeit, it shows some recovery by the possible oil solubilization in anionic surfactants (Kathel and Mohanty, 2013).

Subsequently, the use of functionalized nanomaterials under the reservoir nanoagents concept have also shown a new and increasing potential for oil recovery applications (Kanj, 2013). These demonstrated a strong and positive effect on the rock/fluid interactions with no or little impact on the permeability. Properly functionalized particles can support the disjoining pressure at the three-phase contact line of oil/rock/water and increase the sweep efficiency (Kanj et al., 2020; Wasan and Nikolov, 2003). Several mechanistic studies were demonstrated recently in the energy (oil and gas) field with nanofluids. Some example applications included water tracing, wettability alteration, interfacial tension modification, viscosification, shear-thinning and shear-thickening, fracture healing, stress caging, drilling fluids and reservoir monitoring (Mohamadian et al., 2018). The studies focused most on inorganic nanoparticles application with little to no attention to organic templates (Al-Anssari et al., 2018; Hendraningrat et al., 2013; Hendraningrat and Torsæter, 2014; Karimi et al., 2012; Mcelfresh et al., 2012; Safary and Jamialahmadi, 2013). Moghaddam et al. (2015) studied the effect of several inorganic nanoparticles (ZrO2, CaCO3, TiO2, SiO2, MgO, Al2O3, CeO2) and carbon nanotubes (CNT) on the wettability in carbonate outcrops. They demonstrated more than 20% supplemental recovery over brine in spontaneous imbibition tests at 70 °C (Moghaddam et al., 2015). Li et al. (2017) ran spontaneous imbibition testing at 60 °C on sandstone samples with 10 nm phosphorus-based graphitic carbon nanoparticles in the imbibing brine. They achieved 38% oil recovery with 0.1% of carbon particles in 3% NaCl solution (Li et al., 2017). Nowrouzi et al. (2019) investigated the size effect (10–30 mn) of TiO2 nanoparticles on the imbibition characteristics of carbonate samples. In their study, 70% of the original oil in place, OOIP could be achieved with the smallest (10 nm) particles at the highest tested concentration (1000 ppm) (Nowrouzi et al., 2019). Keykhosravi and Simjoo (2020) employed Gamma-Alumina nanoparticles (10–15 nm) in a spontaneous imbibition study on carbonate outcrops. Despite having some positive impact on the oil recovery, the particles exhibited severe colloidal stability, adsorption/retention, precipitation, and formation damage issues in reservoir conditions of temperature (100 °C) and salinity (150,000–220,000 ppm).

In the past decade, we explored the inexpensive carbon nanodots (CND) as next-generation tracers for reservoir management (Kanj and Kosynkin, 2015; Kanj et al., 2011), foam stability for conformance control (Sakthivel et al., 2019), and to some extent, static wetting and dynamic wettability alteration for oil mobility and recovery (Kanj et al., 2020). The 3–8 nm CNDs are composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen. The CND template is featured by its one-pot synthesis, hydrophilicity, inherent fluorescence, high detectability down to single-digit ppb concentrations, colloidally stability in harsh reservoir environments in temperature and salinity, and biocompatibility. More notably, the CND synthesis is very amenable for scale-up to the kilogram level in a non-industrial laboratory environment. Kanj et al. (2011) synthesized five kilograms of the material using a micro-reactor with ease. Kanj and Kosynkin (2015) scaled up the synthesis to a one-ton quantity (one thousand Kilogram) in collaboration with a chemicals manufacturer at a very affordable cost in a very short period of time.

The average wettability is assessed using the Amott–Harvey or the USBM imbibition tests. Many wettability controlling parameters are known to exist (e.g., brine composition, mineralogy, oil type, permeability, initial water saturation, aging, viscosity ratio, temperature, and pressure). Accordingly, it is advantageous to correlate Amott–Harvey and USBM imbibition with NMR or computerized tomography (CT) analyses. The quantification of wettability in porous media is a challenging task. NMR is employed to acquire qualitative and quantitative information on the aggregated wettability of the rock system (Singh et al., 2021; Valori and Nlcot, 2019). It is a rapid, easy to use, and non-invasive technique (Tinni et al., 2015; Meng et al., 2016; Lai et al., 2018; Dillinger and Esteban, 2014; Zhou et al., 2019). It also helps evaluate the pore structure, pore size distribution, fluids distribution in the porous media, and (oil/brine/rock) wettability.

Previous NMR-based imbibition studies (Zhou et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2016; Al Harbi et al., 2017) considered, among others, brine, surfactant, mineralogy, and pore size effects. In these, it is noted that macropores (pore sizes larger than
3
μ m
with a T2 relaxation in the range of 100–1000 ms) contribute to the majority of oil production. By comparison, the contribution to recovery is much lower from the mesopores (pore sizes in the range of 50 nm to
3
μ m
with a T2 relaxation in the range of 1–100 ms) and almost non-existent from the micropores (pore sizes smaller than 50 nm with a T2 relaxation below 1 ms) (Yang et al., 2016). Recovery further depends on the pore surface roughness and pore connectivity of the rock matrix. Also, in some studies, the frontal displacement itself is very limited for tight rock samples, due to the poor diffusive nature of the displacing fluid (Zhou et al., 2019). Only limited NMR imbibition studies were reported on carbonate samples with nanoparticles. Al Harbi et al. (2017) ran coreflood tests with petroleum sulfonate nanosurfactant (salt surfactant) for oil recovery in carbonate samples. They inferred the oil saturation from NMR after each stage of flooding. They assessed a 7% incremental recovery with 2000 ppm (0.2% w/v) of 0.4 pore volume slug of the salt surfactant injection over the neat seawater injection (Al Harbi et al., 2017). Li et al. (2018) employed a highly concentrated (10,000 ppm) Silica nanofluid (10–20 nm) in 3 wt% NaCl solution in spontaneous imbibition tests on sandstone samples at 60 °C. They reported a 25% incremental recovery over the brine solution (Li et al., 2018). Zhou et al. (2019) further extended on the Li et al. (2018) study using NMR imaging techniques. The NMR study supported the advantage of the Silica nanofluid over the neat NaCl solution (Zhou et al., 2019).

Recently, several researchers investigated the capillarity effect of silica nanoparticles for imbibing into oil-filled sandstone pores and supporting the oil mobility (Wasan and Nikolov, 2003; Hammond and Unsal, 2011, 2009; Kondiparty et al., 2011, 2012; Liu et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2014). They concluded that recovery is influenced by the oil/water interfacial tension, wettability alteration, and wedging phenomena (structural disjoining pressure) at the three-phase contact line. Therefore, it is essential to study the spontaneous imbibition characteristics of nanofluids into the carbonate rock matrix for oil recovery performance.

In all these tests, there is no comparative characterization of the wettability phenomenon on the carbonate samples between nanofluids and surfactants. The mineral composition of sandstones is different from carbonates. Unlike sandstones, carbonates are generally oil-wet. The recovery of oil from carbonate formations is far more challenging due to scale and heterogeneities. In addition, many studies employ model oil for its accessibility. In such studies, the real oil–brine and oil–rock interactions are essentially omitted because of the absence of the polarity of the model oil.

The objective of this study is to understand the efficacy of the spontaneous imbibition characteristics of seawater with additives in oil-saturated carbonate rocks and monitor the fluids saturation and oil recovery at 100 °C over time. We investigated two additives, namely, carbon nanodot (CND) and Gemini surfactant (GS8). Spontaneous imbibition characteristics were studied as a function of the type and concentration of additives in the imbibing seawater, aging of oil-saturated samples, initial water saturation, and sample permeability. The oil and water saturation and wettability were monitored for some key samples using low field NMR. NMR T
2
relaxation measurements provided qualitative estimations of the wettability at different stages of the imbibition process. It allowed characterizing recovery responses from micro-, meso- and macro-pores. Similarly, the two-dimensional T
2
D NMR was used for quantitative fluid (oil and water) typing in the rock matrix at different stages of the imbibition process. A strong correlation was observed between the elaborate imbibition test results of the Amott cell and the fast NMR measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials: Crude oil, nanodots, surfactants, and brine fluids
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the crude oil was obtained from a Saudi Arabian oil field. The physicochemical properties of the oil sample are listed in Table 1. Crude oil contains more aromatics (50%) and saturates (36.2%), and a considerable amount of resins (11%) and asphaltenes (2.8%). Carbon nanodots, CND (Sakthivel et al., 2019; Krysmann et al., 2012), and quaternary ammonium Gemini surfactant, GS8 (Hussain et al., 2019), were synthesized and purified as in the literature. CNDs are 3–8 nm in size and highly fluorescent, very detectable, and well-tuned for colloidal stability in harsh reservoir conditions. These were tested for tracer application in the field (Kanj and Kosynkin, 2015; Kanj et al., 2011). The CND synthesis is very simple and scalable (Kanj et al., 2011, 2009). Similarly, GS8 is also found to be stable at high-temperature and high-salinity (Hussain et al., 2019). However, unlike CND, GS8 is more complex to synthesize and more expensive to produce. GS8 is employed here for comparison with the more abundant and inexpensive CND. Seawater (SW) and formation brine (FW) compositions are detailed in Table 2. Density and viscosity data for different CND and GS8 concentrations are presented in Table 3.

Two sets of Indiana limestone outcrop core plugs with 45 and 250 mD average gas permeabilities and around 20% porosity were employed in this study (Table 4). Disc-shaped samples were also prepared for rock–oil–brine contact angle measurements (Table 5).

Initially, all the samples were cleaned thoroughly using Soxhlet extraction method with toluene and methanol, then dried at 100 °C for 1–2 days. Gas permeability and porosity were measured using the Automated Helium Porosimeter and Permeameter (APP-608) based on Boyle’s law. Brine and oil permeabilities were measured using a coreflood setup in preparation for the spontaneous imbibition testing (Table 4).

Disc-shaped samples were cleaned using Soxhlet extraction and saturated with 100% oil under vacuum, then aged in the same oil at 100 °C for two weeks. In this study, the inverted Sessile drop method was employed to identify the initial state of the wettability by measuring the static contact angle. We also studied wettability on oil-saturated samples at S
wi
. The difference was minimal with only 3%–5% fluctuation. Hence, we considered only 100% oil-saturated disc samples for wettability studies.

Spontaneous imbibition testing involved using up to four Amott setups (e.g., Fig. 1). Tests were run in parallel with different imbibing fluids that consisted of SW (TDS = 67,500 ppm) with varying concentrations of CND and or GS8. The cells were acquired from Vinci Technologies. These are rated for 120 °C and 30 psi. Each cell allows for up to 20 ± 0.1 cc of displaced oil to be measured (Fig. 1). Each test continued for two months. A plateau in oil recovery is reached in all tests during this period. A total of 35 imbibition experiments were performed on five (5) core plug samples, as listed in Table 6.

Tests on the distinctive effect of CND and GS8 concentration (0, 10, 200, and 500 ppm) in SW on the same unaged sample # 1 are presented in Table 6 (Tests# 1–7). Experiments with neat seawater (containing no additives) are baseline experiments. The oil recovery at the 200 ppm concentration level proved optimal for CND and GS8. Accordingly, we included tests that combine both in the imbibing SW base fluid at this concentration (test# 8). Tests 1–8 of Table 6 were performed sequentially on the same sample. After each test, the sample was thoroughly cleaned using the Soxhlet extraction method in preparation for the next test. Tests 9–16 of Table 6 are repeat experiments of Tests 1–8 on a sister sample (#2) with the sample aged for 14 days at 100 °C in each test. The effect of aging is completed with Tests 17–24 of Table 6, with a comparative look at 0, 7, 14, and 42 days aging of the same sample (#3) with only CND in SW as the imbibition fluid. Tests 25–32 of Table 6 examine the effect of the initial FW saturation in the sample on imbibition and recovery. Tests were run in parallel on samples# 1–4 according to Table 6. The remainder of the tests in Table 6 presents the response in a tight sample with much lower permeability with and without additives in SW.

In continuation with the previous spontaneous imbibition experiments, samples were also studied with low field magnetic NMR (2.2 MHz Oxford Geospec 2 rock core analyzer) to monitor the wettability alteration at various stages of the imbibition process in different fluids. Using NMR, the rate of the invading capacity of the imbibing fluids was monitored more accurately and estimated the efficacy of neat SW, CND in SW, and GS8 in SW for wettability alteration. Test# 9 (SW), Test# 11 (200 ppm of CND in SW), and Test# 14 (200 ppm of GS8 in SW) were selected for NMR study on the aged outcrop Sample# 2. A series of T
2
distribution (transverse relaxation time) and T
2
D diffusion mapping were conducted at several stages of imbibition to observe oil/water phase distribution in relation to various imbibing fluids and pore types. NMR measurements are recorded at different stages like after (i) saturation with formation brine, (ii) saturation with oil at S
wi
, (iii) aging, (iv) 1-day imbibition, (v) 10-day imbibition, and (vi) 40-day imbibition.

The well-known Car–Purcell–Meiboom–Gill (CPMG) pulse sequence was used to acquire the relaxation distribution of all the experiments. A 25,000 echo number captured the entire decay with an echo time of 0.1 ms and a signal-to-noise ratio of 100 or better. CPMG measures T
2
relaxation time very efficiently. It is widely used in NMR logging services. The governing equation for T
2
relaxation of a fluid confined in porous media is given according to Brownstein and Tarr (1979):
(1)
1
T
2
=
1
T
2 , b u l k
+
1
T
2 , surface
+
1
T
2 , diffusion
=
1
T
2 , b u l k
+
ρ
2
S
V
+
1
12
D
γ G
t
E
2
where
ρ
2
is the surface relaxivity constant caused by T
2
relaxation enhancement from surface interactions; S/V is the surface-to-volume ratio of the pores; D is the confined fluid diffusion coefficient; γ is the gyromagnetic ratio of the proton, 1H, nuclei (2.675 × 108 rad s−1 T−1); G is the internal magnetic field gradient, and
t
E
is the echo time between the applied 180° pulses. Further details about this methodology can be found somewhere else (Hollingsworth and Johns, 2003).

Two-dimensional NMR mapping, such as T
2
D and T
1
T2 provides more information to quantify the oil, water and gas in the rock matrix and supports well-logging (Hürlimann and Venkataramanan, 2002). Particularly, T
2
D mapping helps separate the water signal from the oil signal for more accurate fluid typing (Chen et al., 2004). The percentage of the oil and water saturations can be quantified from the intensities of the corresponding signals. A detailed description of this methodology can be found in Hürlimann and Venkataramanan (2002).

An interfacial Tensiometer (IFT-700 from Vinci Tech) was used to measure the rock–oil–brine contact angle (invert sessile drop) and the oil–brine interfacial tension (pendant drop). The experimental procedure in regards to these measurements is detailed elsewhere (Kanj et al., 2020). Measurements were made at atmospheric conditions (25 °C and 14.7 psig).

The first phase of this investigation portrays the Amott cell spontaneous imbibition tests. In this study, we considered the effect of additive type (CND, GS8), additives concentration in the imbibing SW (0, 10, 200, 500 ppm), aging of the oil-saturated samples (0, 7, 14, 42 days), initial water saturation (0, 16, 32, 45%), and the rock permeability (45, 250 mD) on the spontaneous imbibition characteristics and oil mobility and recovery. Low field NMR T
2
and T
2
D measurements were taken at different stages of the imbibition to support both a qualitative and a quantitative assessment of the wettability alteration in the carbonate samples. On the other hand, the rock–oil–brine surface contact angle and oil–brine interfacial tension measurements helped shed lights on the oil recovery mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Additive concentration effects on an unaged sample
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2 depicts the 2-month spontaneous imbibition and oil recovery responses for the unaged Sample# 1 using different concentrations of CND and GS8 in SW. This Indiana limestone sample has a good gas permeability of about 250 mD. Tests# 1–8 of Table 6 were run sequentially on the same core plug and at the same initial water saturation, S
wi
(16 ± 1%), to isolate the different effects. The S
wi
level was carefully monitored using optimized settings in the centrifuge.

A baseline experiment was run with neat SW as imbibing fluid yielding a total recovery value of 38.6% (Fig. 2). In this study, the oil recovery is presented as a percent of the OOIP or S
oi
(initial oil saturation). All incremental recoveries are calculated relative to this baseline on the same sample and at the same testing conditions. Hence, the incremental recovery for any additive is calculated by subtracting the recovery from neat SW imbibition. Kanj et al. (2020) noted that aging increases the oil wetness of the carbonate rocks due to the asphaltene in the crude oil depositing on the rock surface over time at high temperature. The wettability of the unaged oil-saturated rock was found to be oil-wet or weakly oil-wet based on a measured rock–oil–water contact angle of 133°. Accordingly, a faster recovery response is noted for the unaged Sample# 1. A plateau in the response is reached within the first 10 h for all tests with only GS8 in the SW and within the first 100 h for the ones with only CND in SW. This rapid early effect for GS8 is obviously due to its surfactancy (more effective in lowering IFT) and molecular (more diffusive) nature. For both additives, an increase in the concentration of the additive in the imbibition fluid seems to transpire with an increase in the ultimate recovery. Moreover, the CND response matched that of the baseline SW for nearly the first 10 h but incrementally exceeded it at all times afterward until a plateau is reached. The incremental recovery for CND in SW over neat SW is 5.76%, 21.2%, and 23.3% for 10, 200, and 500 ppm respectively. Comparatively, the incremental recovery for the GS8 in SW imbibing fluid is very close to that of CND with reported values as 4%, 21.83%, and 24.6% for 10, 200, and 500 ppm respectively. The difference in the ultimate incremental recovery between the more expensive GS8 and the cheaper CND varied from −1.76% at the lowest additive concentration (10 ppm) to 1.3% at the highest additive concentration (500 ppm). CND even demonstrated a small advantage at the lowest concentration tested. And though GS8 exhibits early faster recovery response, it seems to converge with CND over time. We also investigated adding equal amounts of 200 ppm of CND and GS8 in the imbibing SW (Table 6, Test# 8). The combined effect shows a clear early advantage but a small ultimate advantage in incremental recovery above the ones reached by the highest tested concentrations (500 ppm) for each of the additives.

An identical set of experiments to the ones performed on Sample# 1 was repeated on a 2-week aged sister carbonate sample (Sample# 2). As in the previous case, all spontaneous imbibition tests were done here sequentially on the same core plug, and each carried out for 2 months. These constituted Tests# 9–16 of Table 6 with responses depicted in Fig. 3. In this case, the baseline recovery with the neat SW amounted to 34.2%, reflecting a drop of 4.4% from the unaged sample due to the stronger oil-wetness characteristics resulting from aging the sample. While one notices an early time advantage for GS8 over CND in the unaged sample, this advantage almost disappears with aging, as depicted in Fig. 3. The onset of recovery (and imbibition) in Fig. 3 seems to start a few to several hours into testing, with a plateau response taking nearly 500 h or 21 days. By comparison, the onset of recovery and reaching a plateau is up to 50 times faster for the unaged Sample# 1. In this case, the difference in plateau levels between GS8 and CND in SW was −1.9%, 0.9%, and 0.2% for 10, 200, and 500 ppm, respectively. This negative or near zero difference further emphasizes the efficiency of CND as a wettability modifier. The combined effect of mixing the two additives in SW at 200 ppm concentration do not match the unaged sample’s performance. While it nearly matched the performance of the GS8 at 200 ppm for the first 70 h, it diverted closer to the performance of the CND at 200 ppm as the test progressed. Shortly after the 21-day mark, the combined additive response plateaued at 57.3%, below the individual plateau level of either GS8 in SW (59.5%) or CND in SW (58.6%).

The previous Tests# 1–16 (of Table 6) involved using two sister samples (#1 and #2). For time efficiency, Tests# 1–8 and Tests# 9–16 (Table 6) were run in parallel. A third sister sample, Sample# 3, helped further investigate the 7 days and the 42 days aging effects as well as validate the repeatability of the spontaneous imbibition responses for CND in SW at its optimal 200 ppm concentration level (Fig. 4). Tests# 17–24 were done in parallel with the two sets from Figs. 2 and 3. The ultimate recovery values from Fig. 4 are 24.6%, 35.35%, 37.3%, and 40.5% for SW and 51.35%, 58.6%, 60.1%, and 61.7% for 200 ppm CND in SW with 0, 7, 14, and 42 days aging, respectively. Obviously, there is good repeatability of these tests for the unaged (Sample# 1) and the 2-week aging (Sample# 2) responses. The 7 days aging response is noticeably closer to that of the 14 days aging than unaged samples. Unaged samples exhibited a fast early recovery response in the first 10 h of the test. Moreover, aging for the longer 42 days severely impacted the recovery with a drop of 7.3% and 10.75% from the 14 days aged samples for CND in SW and neat SW, respectively. The onset of recovery is also delayed with aging. The time for the onset of recovery increased from nearly 5 min for unaged samples to 15–20 h for the ones aged for 42 days.

The effect of changing the initial water saturation, S
wi
in the core plug at the start of the test with and without CND in the imbibing SW is presented in Fig. 5 (see Tests# 25–32 of Table 6). The objective of the study is to assess the efficacy of the 200 ppm solution of CND in SW in altering the static wettability of the rock and influencing the imbibition process and oil recovery at varying S
wi
levels. The oil-saturated Sample# 4 at the specific S
wi
was aged for 2 weeks at 100 °C ahead of every test. The capillary pressure drives the water imbibition process in water-wet reservoirs. It resists imbibition in oil-wet reservoirs. Our samples are strongly oil wet. For S
wi
= 0 %
, oil saturation is performed on dry core plugs. Under this condition, oil-wetting is at its highest levels for the same aging period and accordingly, the imbibition rate is slowest and the onset of recovery is most delayed for the sample. For this strongly oil-wet (S
wi
= 0 %
) case, the dynamics of wettability alteration is small over the time scale of the imbibition experiment (e.g., see Kanj et al., 2020). This results in weaker added recovery values of only 8% over neat SW.

The oil-wetness reduces with an increase of S
wi
. The wettability shifts to weakly oil-wet with an increase of S
wi
. At higher values of S
wi
, the connate water brine film between the rock and oil interfaces is relatively thicker for high S
wi
case than the low S
wi
(Jiménez-Ángeles and Firoozabadi, 2016). And the asphaltene deposition on the rock surface (or the degree of oil wetness) is lower for higher S
wi
. The water imbibing capacity increases with an increase of water-wetness due to the wettability alteration. Thereby, the (adhesion) work needed to desorb an oil blob is lower for higher S
wi
.
Thus, the time to onset of recovery decreases with increased S
wi
. It dropped from 50 h or more for cases with zero initial water saturation in the sample to 5 h or less at the higher values of S
wi
. The ultimate recovery improved with higher S
wi
and the addition of the CND in SW. By increasing the S
wi
from 0% to 16%, the ultimate recovery improved by 20% and 35% in neat SW and 200 ppm CND in SW, respectively. Moreover, the ultimate recovery improved between 8% and 23% with the addition of CND to SW at all tested levels of S
wi
. Kanj et al. (2020) explored the wedging phenomena effect at the three-phase contact line for the CND in SW. They indicated that while CND reduces the IFT only marginally, it improves the water wetness noticeably. They also noted that the wettability shift with the CND is a result of a capillary pressure reduction and a disjoining pressure enhancement (Kanj et al., 2020).

The effect of permeability variation was studied using Sample# 2 (250 mD) and Sample# 5 (45 mD). Fig. 6 shows this effect on the imbibition characteristics with and without additives (200 ppm CND or 200 ppm GS8) in the imbibing SW. All tests were performed with samples aged for 14 days and an initial water saturation of 16%. Obviously, there is a reduced rate of recovery and reduced total recovery with reduced permeability. There is a strong effect for both CND and GS8 on the recovery over neat SW. More importantly, there seem to be a stronger effect of GS8 on the recovery potential from the lower permeability samples. The difference in oil-recovery plateau levels between GS8 and CND in the imbibing SW amounted to 0.9% and 4.7% for the 250 mD and 45 mD samples, respectively. Again, this can be attributed to the surfactancy and molecular nature of GS8. While CND is more efficient than GS8 in altering wettability, it is less efficient in lowering the IFT. On the other hand, CND particles are 5–10 nm in size and accordingly much less diffusive than the molecular GS8 and can be relatively blind to the dead pockets in the rock matrix.

This section discusses the qualitative and quantitative wettability measurements of the carbonate sample studied using low field NMR. Three tests of spontaneous imbibition were chosen from Section 3.1.2 to study with three different imbibing fluids (SW, 200 ppm of CND in SW, and 200 ppm of GS8 in SW) on the same 2-week aged carbonate sample. T
2
distribution and T
2
D measurements helped monitor the recovery at different stages of the imbibition process. The stages of interest for NMR study included (i) after saturating the sample with formation brine, (ii) after saturating the core with oil at S
wi
, (iii) after aging the oil-saturated sample, (iv) at 1-day imbibition, (v) at 10-day imbibition, and (vi) at 40-day imbibition.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Qualitative wettability assessment by T
2
distribution
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 7a depicts the T
2
distribution of the bulk fluids (oil, formation water, seawater, 200 ppm CND in SW, and 200 ppm GS8 in SW). All brine fluids and solutions presented almost identical and undistinguishable T
2
distribution response in black. Hence, only the formation brine response is presented. Fig. 7a shows that the T
2
response of the brine is characterized by a single sharp peak at 2.32 s. Due to its wide range of composition, the bulk crude oil exhibited three separate broader peaks (than the ones observed for the FW) at 0.1931, 0.0334, and 0.0027 s. A similar response was observed by Freedman et al. (2001) and Freedman and Heaton (2004).

In the same way, T2D measurements of the bulk brine and crude oil were studied at ambient conditions. A higher T2D diffusion coefficient was measured for the brine in comparison with the oil. Fig. 7b shows the measured T2D diffusion coefficient values of 2.
42
×
10
−9 m2 s−1 and 4.
24
×
10
−10 m2 s−1 for the bulk brine and the bulk crude oil, respectively.

Fig. 8 depicts the T
2
distribution of the same carbonate sample (Sample# 2) for different imbibing fluids at varying stages of sample preparation and testing. Measurements were made (i) after saturating the sample with formation brine, (ii) after saturating the sample with oil at S
wi
, (iii) after aging the oil-saturated sample for 2 weeks, (iv) at 1-day imbibition, (v) at 10-day imbibition, and (vi) at 40-day imbibition. The imbibing fluids included SW (Test# 9), 200 ppm of CND in SW (Test# 11), and 200 ppm of GS8 in SW (Test# 14).

T2 distribution of formation brine saturated samples, stage (i), provided a qualitative pore size representation of these samples. Samples presented a multimodal pore size representation with one minor peak and two major peaks (at the higher T
2
values). Peaks at higher T
2
values represent larger pores (Meng et al., 2016; Lai et al., 2018; Dillinger and Esteban, 2014). Macropores (larger than
3
μ m
), mesopores (in the order of 50–3000 nm), and micropores (below 50 nm) are represented by T
2
values in the ranges 0.1–1 s, 0.001–0.1 s, and 0.0001–0.001 s, respectively (Zhou et al., 2019). While macropores governed the pore distribution in the system with 53%–56%, micropores presented the least response contribution at only 0.8–1.5%. This multimodal behavior is a representation of a primary intergranular pore system (macropores), a secondary intragranular pore system (mesopores), and a marginal micropore system (Lai et al., 2018; Dillinger and Esteban, 2014). T
2
measurement in stage (ii) after saturating the sample with crude oil at the irreducible water saturation (S
wi
= 16
%) is presented in Fig. 8. In this case, the entire response shifts slightly to the left towards smaller T
2
values as the pores get filled with the higher viscosity oil that substituted the formation brine. Obviously, oil-saturated samples (Fig. 8, solid black lines) present peaks that are shifted towards a shorter T2 than that of the bulk oil (Fig. 7a, solid red line). This faster relaxation is attributed to the oil–rock​ interaction. Also, this oil–rock interaction is higher than the brine-rock interaction leading to a faster T2 relaxation. Aging the oil-saturated sample for 14 days in stage (iii) further increases the oil–rock interaction due to the deposition of the heavier components of oil (asphaltenes) on the rock surface with a further shift of the response to the left (Freedman and Heaton, 2004).

T
2
distribution responses at one day, ten days, and 40 days of the spontaneous imbibition process are presented in Fig. 8 for the SW (Fig. 8a), 200 ppm of CND in SW (Fig. 8b), and 200 ppm of GS8 in SW (Fig. 8c) imbibing fluids.

The imbibition at 1-day with SW (Fig. 8a) demonstrates the inception of a new peak at the higher relaxation T
2
values as the SW begins to invade the large pores. As the spontaneous imbibition process continues, the peak becomes more defined and nears the bulk brine response curve. The 40-day response curve demonstrates only a slight improvement of water wetness in both macropore and mesopore systems with the SW. One can note a minor invasion of the imbibing SW into these systems. In contrast, no distinguishable changes can be seen in the micropores for the T
2
relaxation response over time. Hence, the micropores system remains mainly oil-wet and practically untouched by the SW.

Fig. 8b depicts a noticeably different behavior with the CND in SW imbibing fluid over neat SW and exhibits a larger and more distinct water peak for the macropores. This indicates that CND contributes to altering the wettability in the macropore system from a strongly oil-wet state to an intermediate- or mixed-wet state. The slight shift of the peak to the right (at larger T
2
values) in reference to the response for the brine saturated sample is an indication that the water behaves as a free fluid in the macropores and still a thin layer of oil covers the walls in this system. The secondary T
2
peaks in the 40-day imbibition response for medium and small pores match the same peak positions in the response for the brine saturated sample. Hence, CND contributes also to a strong positive shift of wettability in the micro- and mesopores with an obvious strong impact on the overall recovery.

The response with the GS8 in SW imbibing fluid of Fig. 8c is shown to be similar to the one in Fig. 8b however, with several differentiating characteristics. GS8 contributes to a good invasion of the water into both the macropore and the mesopore systems. Also, there is a larger shift to the right and divergence from the corresponding peaks in the brine saturated sample indicating possibly a lesser interaction with the pore wall with more freedom to move.

As observed earlier, the recovery from imbibing GS8 in SW is higher than either CND in SW or neat SW. One can note from Fig. 8c that the micropore system contributes very little to the recovery. This indicates that the wettability alteration and recovery with GS8 in SW occur most likely in the larger mesopore and macropore systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Quantitative saturation distribution by T
2
distribution
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition to the qualitative assessments from the T
2
relaxation measurements, we also performed quantitative T
2
D measurements at different stages of the spontaneous imbibition process. This helped validate the material balance estimates as obtained from the Amott test. T
2
D measurements were made for all studied imbibing fluids and at all stages of sample preparation (brine-saturated, oil-saturated, and aged) and spontaneous imbibition testing (1-day, 10-day, and 40-day). Measurement at different imbibition stages provided imbibition capacities of each test fluid. All measurements are summarized in Fig. 9 (neat SW), Fig. 10 (CND in SW), and Fig. 11 (GS8 in SW). Diffusion coefficients in the ranges of 1.88-2 × 10−9, 2.65–2.98 × 10−10, and 2.36 × 10−10 m2 s−1 are measured for the brine-saturated, oil-saturated, and aged samples, respectively. These represent lower diffusion values than those noted for the bulk fluids due to the higher surface relaxivity caused by the pore confinement and wetting in the rock. Aging for two weeks of the oil-saturated sample caused a further reduction in the diffusion due to the increased oil-wetness as a result.

Table 7 presents a summary of the T
2
D measurements in terms of recovery as provided in Figs. 9–11. The same table also compares these to the values obtained from the Amott cell experiments. The same trend is preserved between the two approaches in estimating the oil recovery.

It is also noted in Figs. 10 and 11 that the oil diffusion coefficients at 40-day imbibition is lower in GS8 (1.768 × 10−10 m2 s−1) than CND (2.65 × 10−10 m2 s−1). This supports a lower recovery from micropores with GS8 than CND.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle and interfacial tension measurements
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the oil recovery mechanisms with CND and GS8, we measured the rock–oil–brine wettability and the oil–brine interfacial tension as a function of the additive type (either CND or GS8) and its concentration in SW. The static wetting effect is presented in Fig. 12. CND demonstrates a more dominant wettability influence than GS8. However, its ability to reduce the IFT is small by comparison to that of GS8. Kanj et al. (2020) tested CND concentrations up to 10,000 ppm for impact on the oil–water IFT. They observed that CND reduces IFT only marginally even in high concentrations. Kanj et al. (2020) also confirmed that the CND contributes to recovery with a stronger influence in altering wettability than lowering interfacial tension. Fig. 12 depicts the direct comparison of wettability alteration and interfacial tension changes with their effect on oil recovery. Both CND and GS8 demonstrate a good correlation for the oil recovery with wettability alteration and IFT.

The dynamic wettability alteration of the rock sample is in line with the well-known low salinity effect on oil recovery. The salinity contrast between the connate water in the core plug and the imbibing seawater is in the order of 4. (There is also a change in the ionic composition between the two brines.) This salinity contrast between FW and SW creates a dynamic reduction of the contact angle and a favorable shift in the wettability. The capillary pressure of the imbibing water would increase with the increase of water-wetness leading to oil displacement and recovery. This positive effect is magnified further with the adding of the CND and GS8 in the SW.

While CND reduces the oil–water interfacial tension only marginally, it improves the water-wetness of the carbonate rock noticeably. The particles do not adsorb to or interact with the rock surface (Kanj et al., 2011) and accordingly do not influence the capillary size. Following the Young–Laplace relation, this elevates the capillary forces as the main contributor to wettability alteration for CND. Kanj et al. (2020) demonstrated that the Carbon Nanodots alter wettability by reducing the pressure in the oil capillaries and improving the disjoining pressure at the three-phase (rock–oil–brine) contact line. As such, wettability alteration dominates the recovery mechanism with CND. What drives CND to migrate in and out of the dead pockets and micropores to unlock this oil potential? The answer comes from the well-studied coupled flow phenomena and in particular, phenomena related to electrokinetics. A chief candidate here is diffusiophoresis (Shin et al., 2016; Prieve et al., 2019). Diffusiophoresis refers to the locomotion of charged nanoparticles induced by a salinity gradient. Salinity gradients occur naturally during secondary and tertiary recovery operations as an access to the dead pockets containing oil and the hypersaline connate water is opened. This takes place at the interface between the lower salinity injected seawater (TDS of about 57,000 ppm) and the formation brine (TDS of 150,000 to 220,000 ppm) in an oil reservoir. High salinity regions are commonly micropore regions with untapped oil and governed with small capillaries. Kanj and Sakthivel (2021) presented experiments that validated the diffusiophoresis concept for a reservoir environment and demonstrated that CND would indeed migrate up the salinity gradient inside the rock matrix (Kanj and Sakthivel, 2021). More importantly, the combination of improved disjoining pressure, reduced capillary pressure, and diffusiophoresis constitute a driver for CND to displace “difficult” oil from the micropores in particular (Fig. 8b) as well as other pore systems.

On the other hand, the recovery process transpires differently with GS8. Due to its strong surface-active properties, GS8 supports recovery through a combination of interfacial tension reduction and wettability alteration. While GS8 alters wettability less efficiently than CND, it proved superior at reducing the oil–brineIFT. In this regard, it targets the “easy” oil in the macro- and meso-pores more efficiently and contributes to faster early recovery of oil than CND (Figs. 3 and 6). Yet, NMR T
2
distributions of Fig. 8c suggest that GS8 is inefficient in recovering and/or mobilizing oil from the micropores. With spontaneous imbibition, the detached oil by GS8 will remain in the micro- (and meso-) pores due to the lack of necessary capillary pressure and less than optimal wettability alteration.

We run spontaneous imbibition tests to study the impact of a Carbon nanodot (CND) and an in-house Gemini Surfactant (GS8) additive in SW on wettability alteration and recovery in oil-wet carbonate rocks. The study assessed the efficiency of the simpler, more abundant, and less expensive CND in dilute concentrations (below 200 ppm) in SW for the targeted application.

The study also addressed the effect of various factors, such as CND and GS8 concentrations (0, 10, 200, and 500) in seawater, aging (0, 7, 14, and 42 days), initial water saturation (0, 16, 32, and 45%), and rock permeability (45 and 250 mD). CND exhibited a greater effect on changing the wetting behavior of the carbonate samples from strongly oil-wet to water-wet and improved the oil recovery performance irrespective of aging, permeability, and initial water saturation. Similar results were obtained for the GS8 surfactant, however, with a stronger tendency on lowering IFT and a faster initial rate of recovery. The ultimate oil recovery was comparable for both additives in the SW with a clear advantage for the CND (CND is easier to synthesize and scaleup than GS8). We also studied the imbibition characteristics of neat SW, CND in SW, and GS8 in SW on the oil-wet carbonate samples using low field NMR by measuring T
2
and T
2
D distributions. This helped assess the recovery in the spontaneous imbibition process both qualitatively and quantitatively. Analysis of the NMR results confirmed that the largest contribution to recovery with GS8 in SW comes from the macropores and only a marginal contribution comes from the micropores. With CND, all pore systems contribute to recovery almost equally. Overall, T
2
D measurements for fluid saturation at different stages of the imbibition agreed with the Amott cell results.

Surface contact angle (rock/oil/brine), IFT (oil/brine), and NMR T
2
and T
2
D measurements supported and further helped explain the spontaneous imbibition outcomes on recovery. These measurements emphasized the mechanistic approach for oil recovery with either one of the additives in SW. For CND, wettability alteration, increased capillary pressure, and enhanced disjoining pressure are the main contributing effects on enhanced recovery. On the other hand, IFT reduction secured an early lead for GS8. Both additives contributed to comparable ultimate recovery values with a faster but later contribution coming from CND.

Overall, both the CND and GS8 demonstrated a positive EOR value for carbonate reservoirs. CND is characterized by its “one-pot” synthesis, ease of scalability, demonstrated stability and mobility, high detectability to single digit ppb levels, and low-cost. This renders CND a viable tool for oifield enhanced recovery operations in carbonate reservoir.
Sivabalan Sakthivel: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Mazen Yousef Kanj: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
